coredata.dc:title: Molybdenum-oxo-sulfide quantum dot-based nanocarrier: Efficient generation of reactive oxygen species via photo/chemodynamic therapy and stimulus-induced drug release

coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of multifunctional nano-therapies has increased gradually to strengthen the therapeutic performance and minimize adverse effects of traditional cancer treatment strategies. Currently, we have designed a facile preparation drug-loaded nanocarrier for multimodal cancer therapy upon external stimuli. First, defect-rich molybdenum oxo-sulfide (MoOxS2-x) quantum dots (QDs) was synthesized via rapid biomineralization techniques with superior optical quantum yield reaching upto 37.28%. The presence of the Fenton ion, Mo+IV/+VI, enables MoOxS2-x QDs to efficiently catalyze peroxide solutions to produce •OH radicals for chemodynamic treatment (CDT) and also deactivate the intracellular glutathione (GSH) enzymes through redox reaction for boosted reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated therapies. In addition, upon laser combination, MoOxS2-x QDs generate ROS for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Also, due to a large amount of sulfide content, MoOxS2-x QDs showed excellent H2S gas release in acidic pH for cancer gas therapy. Then, MoOxS2-x QDs was further conjugated with ROS-responsive thioketal linked Camptothecin (CPT-TK-COOH) drug, forming a multitargeted MoOxS2-xCPT anticancer agent with better drug-loading efficiency (38.8%). After triggering the ROS generation through the CDT and PDT mechanisms, the thioketal linkage was disrupted, releasing up to 79% of the CPT drug in 48 h. Besides, in vitro experiments verified that MoOxS2-x QDs possess higher biocompatibility with 4T1 and HeLa cells but also showed considerable toxicity in the presence of laser/H2O2, resulting in 84.45% cell death through PDT/CDT and chemotherapeutic effects. Therefore, the designed MoOxS2-xCPT exhibited outstanding therapeutic benefits for image-guided cancer therapy.

UV–vis and PL properties of MoOxS2-x QDs. (a) UV–Vis spectra (Abs.), PL excitation (Ex.), and emission spectra (Em.) of MoOxS2-x QDs. PL emission spectra of MoOxS2-x QDs prepared using a different molar ratio of (b) Na2S and (c) l-cysteine. (d) 2D PLE mapping of MoOxS2-x QDs. (e) PL spectra of MoOxS2-x QDs obtained by varying the excitation wavelength. (f) Fluorescence decay lifetime spectra of MoOxS2-x QDs observed with different sulfur contents, (g) Luminescence stability of MoOxS2-x QDs solutions where the photos are captured under UV light and daylight conditions for four weeks. (h) The relative PL intensity of MoOxS2-x QDs measured for two months, where Io and I represent the initial and final PL intensity of MoOxS2-x QDs (mean ± standard deviation; SD, n = 3), (i) A photostability assay of MoOxS2-x QDs based on the emission intensities of MoOxS2-x QDs at various Mo:S ratios, collected under continuous UV lamp illumination (excitation wavelength: 360 nm).

(a) The correlation of photonics-induced ROS-generating activity of MoOxS2-x QDs during the photocatalytic/photodynamic process (ISC: intersystem crossing; So: ground state energy; S1: excited singlet state; T1: excited triplet state) and oxygen-self-sufficient systems for PDT I and PDT II, (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of 1.0 mM DPBF solution containing 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs treated with 1.0 W/cm2 of laser light at various times, (c) Comparison for normalized absorption intensity of DPBF solution (at λ = 416 nm) obtained with and without MoOxS2-x QDs treated using a 532 nm laser light (SD, n = 3). (d) Emission spectra of a 9,10-dimethyl anthracene (DMA) solution containing MoOxS2-x QDs when it was exposed to a 532 nm laser at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, (e) Comparison for the normalized PL intensity of DMA solution treated with laser at λ = 434 nm either in the presence or absence of MoOxS2-x QDs (SD, n = 3). (f) PL spectra of singlet oxygen (1O2) were measured at ∼ 1271 nm using MoOxS2-x QDs solution saturated with O2 gas and irradiated with a 532 nm laser source (1.0 W/cm2) for 10 min.

Peroxidase and GSH depletion property of MoOxS2-x QDs; (a) Schematic diagram for GSH-depletion-enhanced Fenton-like reaction of MoOxS2-x QDs. UV–vis spectra of TMB solution monitored using (b) 50 mM of H2O2, 100 ppm of MoOxS2-x QDs, and various pH conditions (4.0–7.0); (c) utilizing 50 mM of H2O2 and different concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs (0–250 ppm) at pH 5.0; (d) photographic image of concentration-dependent TMB oxidation in the presence of MoOxS2-x QDs (0–150 ppm) and set of reaction pathways occurred between QDs, TMB, and H2O2 solution in the presence of laser irradiation. (e) Time-dependent absorbance spectra of ox-TMB upon the oxidation of 10 mM TMB in the presence of 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs and 50 mM H2O2 and (f) with 100 ppm of MoOxS2-x QDs by varying molar amount of H2O2 solution (0 mM − 100 mM) at pH 5.0. (g) Photographic image of concentration-dependent GSH depletion after incubating with 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs over 4 h and completing the reaction pathways between GSH and the MoVI ion. (h) Absorption spectra of 0.2 mM GSH solution mixed with 100 ppm of MoOxS2-x QDs and 0.4 mM DTNB in PBS (pH 5.0) were measured at different time intervals. (i) Normalized absorption intensity of GSH measured at 412 nm in the absence or presence of MoOxS2-x QDs (SD, n = 3).

UV–vis absorption spectra of 1.5 mM NBT solution coupled (a) without and (b) with 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs, treated using 532 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2) for different time intervals. (c) Comparison of the normalized absorption intensity of NBT solution (at λ 260 nm) coupled with and without MoOxS2-x QDs treated under a 532 nm laser source (SD, n = 3). ESR spectroscopic analysis of (d) 4-oxo-TEMP signals of 1O2 in the presence of MoOxS2-x QDs with and without laser illumination, (e) ESR signal DMPOO• responding to •OH formation in the presence of MoOxS2-x QDs/H2O2 solution with and without laser illumination, and (f) ESR signal DMPOO/DMSO solution for responding to the generation of •O2 – in MoOxS2-x QDs/H2O2, either with or without laser irradiation. All laser illumination experiments were conducted with a 532 nm, 1.0 W/cm2 laser source. (g) Photographic images concentration-dependent of MB+ formation in the presence of MoOxS2-x QDs (0 – 300 ppm) under mildly acidic conditions; (h) Cumulative releasing profile of H2S gas obtained under different acidic conditions in the presence of 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs (SD, n = 3); (i) Calibration curve of the concentration-dependent absorbance plot at 664 nm (R2 = 0.98) for H2S evolution quantification (SD, n = 3).

In vitro drug activity of MoOxS2-xCPT; (a) UV–visible absorption spectra of MoOxS2-x QDs, CPT-TK-COOH, and MoOxS2-xCPT. (b) Standard calibration curve of CPT was measured at λ 366 nm (R2 = 0.98) for drug-related quantification (SD, n = 3). (c) PL emission spectra of CPT-TK-COOH, MoOxS2-xCPT, and MoOxS2-x QDs. (d) TEM image of MoOxS2-xCPT, (e) Zeta potential of MoOxS2-x QDs and MoOxS2-xCPT (SD, n = 3). (f) XPS survey spectra of MoOxS2-xCPT and their corresponding high-resolution spectra for (g) Mo 3d; (h) O 1s; (i) S 2p; (j) N 1s; (k) C 1s. (l) CPT releasing profile from MoOxS2-xCPT prodrug performed in various conditions such as PBS only (pH 7.4 and 5.0), pH 5.0 + H2O2, pH 7.4 + Laser, and pH 5.0 + H2O2 + Laser. The data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD; n = 3). Significant differences at the same drug concentration at *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 are indicated.

Intracellular biocompatibility and uptaking experiment; cellular viabilities of (a) 4T1 and (b) HeLa cells incubated with various concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs (0 – 500 ppm) for 24 and 48 h. (c) In vitro CT evaluation using 4T1 cells incubated with different concentrations of MoOxS2-xCPT (0–100 ppm). (d) Photo-enhanced therapeutic effect of MoOxS2-x QDs; for PDT only, the cells were incubated with MoOxS2-x QDs and irradiated with laser, for PDT/CDT study, the cells were incubated with medium containing MoOxS2-x QDs/H2O2 solution and irradiated with laser, (e) For the synergistic effect of MoOxS2-xCPT, 4T1 cells were incubated with MoOxS2-xCPT prodrug containing H2O2 solution and exposed to the laser. All laser experiments were performed using 532 nm laser light with a power density of 1.0 W/cm2. (f) Cumulative summary for cancer-killing efficacy of 100 ppm MoOxS2-xCPT via a different therapeutic mechanism such as PDT, CDT, CT, and their combined effect. The control experiment has been done using MoOxS2-x QDs without laser and H2O2. (g) Intracellular GSH reduction performance of MoOxS2-x QDs detected at 412 nm by varying concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs. (h) The standard curve of optical density (OD) versus the different concentrations of GSH (recovery test R2 = 0.99) was measured using a DTNB probe at 412 nm. The data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD; n = 3). Significant differences at the same drug concentration at *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 are indicated. (i) Confocal micrography of the cellular uptaking experiment using 4T1 cells stained with NucGreen and incubated with MoOxS2-x QDs; Green fluorescence indicates the location of the nucleus; blue fluorescence conforms MoOxS2-x QDs, and merged images show the superimposed images of green and blue fluorescence. The scale bar is 50 μM.

(a) Confocal images of 4T1 cells incubated with various concentration of MoOxS2-x QDs for 12 h at 37 °C and humidified atmosphere. The cancer cells were stained with DAPI to track the nucleus. The green channel indicates that the DCFH-DA has been oxidized into DCF species after reacting with the ROS generated in the cellular organ system; For the Control channel; 4T1 cells were treated with laser/H2O2 source only; For the L50 and L100 channel; 4T1 cells were incubated with 50 and100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs, respectively, while L50 + H and L100 + H channel; 4T1 cells incubated with 50 and 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs respectively treated with 50 μM H2O2 solution. Then all the cells experiment were irradiated with a 532 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 5 min. (b) Hydrolysis and ROS induced oxidation of DCFH-DA with MoOxS2-x QDs to form DCF green fluorescent material. (c) Cellular imaging of 4T1 cells incubated with 45 μL inhibitors (nystatin, genistein, amiloride, and MβCD) for 30 min, then treated with 100 ppm MoOxS2-xCPT for 3 h. The control experiment was performed without adding an inhibitor. The blue and green channel images were captured using 405 and 488 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively. The merged images show superimposed images of green and blue fluorescence. The scale bar is 50.0 μM. (d) Cellular uptaking mechanism of MoOxS2-xCPT via endocytosis trafficking in an intracellular cancer cell.

In vitro fluorescent images of 4T1 cells treated with different external stimuli; (a) with 10 μM H2O2 and laser in the absence of material (control experiment); (b) in the presence of 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs; (c) with 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs and laser for PDT channel; (d) with 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs, 10 μM H2O2, and laser for PDT/CDT channel; (e) with 100 ppm MoOxS2-xCPT and laser to evaluate PDT/CT effect; (f) with 100 ppm MoOxS2-xCPT and 10 μM H2O2, then subsequently irradiated using a laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 5 min to assess the synergistic effect of /PDT/CDT/CT channel. All the laser experiments used a 532 nm of 1.0 W/cm2 power density. The green color was obtained from the NucGreen staining agent (488 nm excitation wavelength). The blue color represents the material's fluorescent image uptake within the cells (405 nm excitation wavelength). The red luminescence represents that PI molecules interacted with the nucleic acid of the dead cells. The images were obtained using a 561 nm excitation wavelength. The merge channels were collected after overlaying NucGreen, Material fluorescent, and PI cellular images. Scale bars are 50 μm.

Schematic illustration of the bottom-up synthesis of MoOxS2-x QDs at room temperature and the synthesis of ROS-responsive MoOxS2-xCPT: Firstly, MoOxS2-x QDs are synthesized through the biomineralization method in the presence of Na2S and l-cysteine solution where molybdenum trioxide is used as the molybdate precursor, next ROS-cleavable thioketal linkers (CPT-TK-COOH) are synthesized from thioketal linker (TK-COOH) and Camptothecin (CPT). MoOxS2-xCPT is fabricated using MoOxS2-x QDs and CPT-TK-COOH with EDC/NHS used as the ROS-stimulated CPT releasing mechanism a fabrication process using as the ROS-stimulated, and systemic delivery of MoOxS2-xCPT is used as a versatile therapeutic platform for efficient intracellular induction of multimodal synergistic treatments based on chemotherapy, photodynamic therapy, the Fenton-like reaction, GSH depletion, and H2S evolution resulting in superior induced cancer cell death efficacy.
Comparison of the properties of MoOxS2-x QDs and quinine sulfate 1:1.5 (Mo:S).

head.ce:title: Molybdenum-oxo-sulfide quantum dot-based nanocarrier: Efficient generation of reactive oxygen species via photo/chemodynamic therapy and stimulus-induced drug release

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of multifunctional nano-therapies has increased gradually to strengthen the therapeutic performance and minimize adverse effects of traditional cancer treatment strategies. Currently, we have designed a facile preparation drug-loaded nanocarrier for multimodal cancer therapy upon external stimuli. First, defect-rich molybdenum oxo-sulfide (MoOxS2-x) quantum dots (QDs) was synthesized via rapid biomineralization techniques with superior optical quantum yield reaching upto 37.28%. The presence of the Fenton ion, Mo+IV/+VI, enables MoOxS2-x QDs to efficiently catalyze peroxide solutions to produce •OH radicals for chemodynamic treatment (CDT) and also deactivate the intracellular glutathione (GSH) enzymes through redox reaction for boosted reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated therapies. In addition, upon laser combination, MoOxS2-x QDs generate ROS for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Also, due to a large amount of sulfide content, MoOxS2-x QDs showed excellent H2S gas release in acidic pH for cancer gas therapy. Then, MoOxS2-x QDs was further conjugated with ROS-responsive thioketal linked Camptothecin (CPT-TK-COOH) drug, forming a multitargeted MoOxS2-xCPT anticancer agent with better drug-loading efficiency (38.8%). After triggering the ROS generation through the CDT and PDT mechanisms, the thioketal linkage was disrupted, releasing up to 79% of the CPT drug in 48 h. Besides, in vitro experiments verified that MoOxS2-x QDs possess higher biocompatibility with 4T1 and HeLa cells but also showed considerable toxicity in the presence of laser/H2O2, resulting in 84.45% cell death through PDT/CDT and chemotherapeutic effects. Therefore, the designed MoOxS2-xCPT exhibited outstanding therapeutic benefits for image-guided cancer therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (•O2 –), and singlet oxygen (1O2), significantly suppress cancer cells by breaking biomolecules, inducing cellular apoptosis or necrosis [1,2,3]. With the widespread attention of ROS-based anticancer strategies in recent years, the Fenton-reaction and light-activated photosensitizer are the most commonly used approaches to generating cytotoxic radicals upon the combination with endogenous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and a laser source, respectively [4]. The inappropriate selection of a visible light source and the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes resulted in a low yield of ROS. Due to these factors, the role of photocatalysis reactions in cancer therapeutics for ROS generation has been bounded [5 6]. The classical Fenton reaction can generate adequate ROS; however, low H2O2 concentration in tumor cells has restricted the production of •OH radicals, leading to poor chemodynamic therapeutic efficacy. Therefore, designing a multi-way-strategy to enhance ROS production for effective cancer therapy is highly advised. Metal quantum dots (QDs), the most well-known ROS generator, undergo the light absorption process at different wavelength ranges and facilitate ROS formation via two possible mechanisms: the photocatalytic process and photodynamic therapy (PDT). In the photocatalytic process, QDs generate holes (h+) in the ground state (So) and electrons (e-) in the excited singlet state (S1). These can separately react with water and dissolved oxygen molecules (O2) to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 –), respectively [7 8].

A photosensitizer derived from two-dimensional (2D) transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) has lately received attention in photocatalysis and nanomedicine [9,10]. Briefly, TMDs possess a layered geometry with a general formula, X-M−Y/X-M−X monolayer (M = Transition metal; X, Y = chalcogen; S, Se, Te), with each structure consisting of covalently linked monoatomic thick stacked repeated layers [11 12]. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), in particular, formed a characteristic layered structure (S-M−S) through weak Van der Waals interaction [10,13]. In certain studies, researchers have devised oxygen engineering by swapping one of the sulfide ions in the framework, resulting in S-Mo-O (oxo-sulfide structure). Moreover, the presence of oxygen in molybdenum-oxo-sulfide resulted in the emergence of active sites that could potentially be exploited in catalysis and biological processes [14,15]. By modifying the morphology and chemical composition of as-prepared MoS2 to develop nanosheets (NSs), zero-dimensional (0D) nanodots (NDs), and QDs, fascinating properties in electrical, optical, and catalytic applications can be identified [16,17,18]. Due to the stronger quantum confinement effect and edge effects, the multi-layers of 2D MoS2 might be reduced to a single-layer structure or 0D NDs or QDs [19,20,12]. To emphasize the applicability of MoS2 in biomedical engineering, especially in ROS-based therapeutics, a strategy should be designed to produce fluorescence MoS2 QDs or NDs for integrated image-guided therapeutics. Several studies on the synthesis of fluorescent MoS2 QDs/NDs have recently been released; nevertheless, poor optical and low quantum yield (QYs) and complicated synthesis approaches remain the key challenges in this domain [21,22,23].

Oxygen engineering into MoS2 QDs was recently discovered to improve the optical QYs. For example, after the introduction of molecular oxygen, it is effectively adsorbed into S2- vacancies of MoS2 QDs and stimulates the charge transfer phenomena, leading to the conversion of excitons to Trion. This causes the elimination of surface vacancies and trap states, which commonly result in weak optical properties. In other words, the emergence of defect-tolerated electronic bands significantly improves the optical property of MoS2 QDs [24 25 26]. It has been suggested that a larger quantity of oxygen ion on the surface of MoS2 QDs would result in a higher yield of singlet oxygen and higher binding affinity toward 3O2 [12]. In contrast, the elimination of sulfur ions in MoS2 QDs results in a high density of edge sites [27], which most likely transforms into ROS [28,29]. So far, the synthesis of high-quality MoS2 QDs has been challenging because of lengthy reaction times, poor purification methods, and uncontrolled size and morphology of QDs. Among them, the purification and uncontrollability of nucleation and growth are anticipated to result in low QYs and hinder their potential applicability [30,31,21,22,23]. Recently, biomineralization techniques have emerged as the dominant method for synthesizing TMDs due to their simplicity, rapid reaction time, and particle growth controllability. Post-treatment approaches, such as ligand/heteroatom addition or pH environment manipulation, are crucial for obtaining relatively stable and optically improved MoS2 QDs. With that said, the fabrication of oxygen defect-rich MoS2 QDs with higher optical quality using a simple room temperature synthesis is urgently required to construct ROS-mediated therapeutic for alternative cancer prevention.

Herein, we report the synthesis of defects-tolerant MoOxS2-x QDs, at room temperature using a one-pot biomineralization-assisted approach. The synthesis medium was altered using NaOH/HCl to obtain defect-rich MoOxS2-x QDs, which further conjugated with the thiolate group of l-cysteine through the sulfur vacancies. This strategy significantly stabilized the surface of the QDs and enhanced their QYs to 37.28%, which is eightfold of the previously reported Mo-based QDs. The presence of redox metal ions and defect-rich properties of MoOxS2-x QDs endow them with potential uses in biomedical applications. Upon laser irradiation and H2O2 combination, MoOxS2-x QDs generate ROS for PDT and CDT efficiency. Simultaneously, MoOxS2-x QDs promote GSH reduction and H2S generation owing to the presence of Mo+6/+4 and S2- ions, respectively, for enhanced CDT efficacy and gas therapy of cancer. Then, ROS-responsive drug (camptothecin derivatives, CPT-TK-COOH) was integrated with MoOxS2-x QDs via organic linkers. The resulting material, MoOxS2-xCPT, exhibits high loading and releasing performance upon activation by ROS. In vitro examination of MoOxS2-x QDs confirms that our material owned good biocompatibility towards cancer cells and also caused enormous cell death upon activation by external stimuli through CDT/PDT and chemotherapy mechanisms.

All chemicals and reagents were used as received without any further purification. MoO3 (95%) and Bovine Serum Album (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA). Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, 84%), NaOH (97%), HCl (50%), l-cysteine (98%), anhydrous acetone (99.8%), trichloro benzyl chloride (98%), and triethylamine (TEA, 99%) were obtained from 11 Technology (China). 3-mercaptopropionic acid (99%), trifluoroacetic acid (98%), 4-dimethyl aminopyridine (DMAP, 99%), N -Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), ethyl(dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC, 97%), were also obtained from 11 Technology (China). Dichloromethane (DCM, 99.5%), 30% H2O2 were obtained from Fluka. WST-1 (4-[3-(4-iodophenyl)–2H-5-tetrazolio]-1,3-benzenedisulfonate assay was purchased from Roche Applied Science (Penzberg, Germany). Camptothecin (CPT, TCI, 97%), 3,3,5,5 -tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride hydrate (TMB, 99%), glutathione (GSH, 98%), deuterium oxide (D2O, 99.9%), 5,5 -dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB, 98%), minimum essential medium Engle (MEME), high-glucose Dulbecco s modified Eagle s medium (DMEM, 90%), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.8%), 1,4-diphenyl-2,3-benzofuran, (DPBF, 99.95%) and 9, 10-dimethyl anthracene (DMA, 99%), N, N-dimethyl-p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride (DMPD, 98%), Zinc(II) acetate (99.9%), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 99.8%) were purchased from J.T.baker, USA. The nucleic staining probes such as propidium iodide (PI, 94%), NucGreenTM, and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, 98%) dead cell nucleic acid stain were purchased from ABP Biosciences, USA. 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, ≥99%), 2,7-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA, >97%), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, ≥97%), Quinine hemisulfate salt monohydrate (99%), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.8%) were ordered from Acros, USA. All other chemicals and reagents used were of analytical grade.

In a typical procedure, the molybdate precursor solution was first prepared by dissolving molybdenum trioxide in water and adjusting the pH value to be greater than 10. The resultant solution was transparent and colorless after sonication. Then, 1 mL of the Mo precursor solution was mixed into 40 mL of BSA solution (1 mg/mL), followed by adding 0.3 mL of 0.5 M Na2S·9H2O and 0.2 mL of 0.5 M l-cysteine prepared in H2O by adjusting the pH value to be greater than 10 under vigorous stirring at room temperature. After 30 min, they were filtered using a 0.20 μM filter. Then, the pH value of the mixed solution was subsequently adjusted to 7.75 + 0.25 by adding 2.5 M HCl/NaOH under vigorous stirring at 500 rpm. When the pH value approached 7.75 ± 0.25, a clear light-yellow solution of MoOxS2-x QDs was collected. The effect of the initial molar ratio of Mo and S on the quantum yield of QDs was investigated. The initial reagent Mo:S molar ratio was prepared as described in Table S1 stoichiometric ratio, while other factors were optimized in Table S2 - S3.

The fluorescence quantum yield was determined by measuring a standard solution of quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L-1 H2SO4, which had a quantum yield of 54% at 360 nm. The equations below were employed to calculate the values using the standard reference sample.

D is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength and η is the refractive index. The subscript “q” refers to the reference fluorophore quinine sulfate of known quantum yield. The calculation details are presented in the following Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosynthesis of ROS-cleavable thioketal linkers (TK-COOH) and Camptothecin derivatives (CPT-TK-COOH)
ce:section.ce:para: Following the recently reported literature [32,33], a thioketal linker (TK-COOH) and ROS-responsive CPT-TK-COOH were synthesized. Briefly, 39.68 mmol of anhydrous 3-mercaptopropionic acid, 19.48 mmol of anhydrous acetone, and 11.68 mmol of trifluoroacetic acid, were poured into a sample vial and gently stirred at room temperature for 1 h until crystallization occurred. The resulting TK-COOH was washed with hexane and cold water, filtered through a 0.45 μM filter, freeze-dried, and stored in a refrigerator for future use. 1H NMR spectra (400 MHz, DMSO‑d6 ) δ (ppm): 12.40 (broad, 1H), 2.74 (t, 4H), 2.49 (t, 4H), 1.50 (s, 6H), while FTIR spectra has confirmed the presence of COOH, CH2, CO, –CH3, CS, corresponding to broad peak centered 3250 (3450–2600 cm−1), 2968 cm−1, 1707 cm−1, 1407 and 673 cm−1 of produced TK-COOH (Fig. S1).

To synthesize the ROS-sensitive CPT-TK-COOH, camptothecin was reacted with TK-COOH following modifications of previously reported methods [32,33], and Yamaguchi esterification [34], was used. Specifically, 0.5 mmol of TK-COOH powder was dissolved in 10 mL of DMF, followed by addition of 1.5 mmol triethylamine, 0.5 mmol trichlorobenzoylchloride, and 0.1 mmol DMAP. The mixture was then mixed at room temperature for 10 min, and 0.25 mmol of the CPT solution (87.1 mg CPT, 10 mL DMF) was injected into the mixture and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The reaction was neutralized with DI water before isolation using DCM and saturated brine water. Finally, excess water was removed through suction filtration with sodium sulfate, and the CPT-TK-COOH was concentrated using a rota-evaporator to form a solid, and the crude product was stored in a refrigerator for characterization and subsequent use. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d6 ) δ (ppm): 12.41 (b, 1H), 8.82 (s, 1H), 8.50 (s, 1H), 8.10 (d, 1H), 7.92 (t, 1H), 7.88 (t, 1H), 7.40 (d, 1H), 6.90 (s, 1H), 5.41 (s, 2H), 5.11 (s, 2H), 2.89 (t, 2H), 2.72 (t, 2H), 2.49 (t, 2H), 2.41 (t, 2H), 2.15 (q, 2H), 1.56 – 1.49 (s, 6H), 0.94 (t, 3H) (Fig. S2). In the FTIR spectra (Fig. S2), the presence of COOH stretch, aryl ring, and ester carbonyl confirmed the structure of the synthesized CPT-TK-COOH. The strong peak at 3433 cm−1 indicated the presence of COOH stretch, while the peak at 3270 cm−1 and the overtone in the region of 2000 – 1800 cm−1 confirmed the presence of the aryl ring. The carbonyl peaks in the region of 1736 – 1585 cm−1 indicated the presence of carboxylic acid and ester carbonyl, thereby confirming the structure of synthesized CPT-TK-COOH.

The MoOxS2-xCPT prodrug was fabricated by conjugating the carboxylic group of thioketal linker-camptothecin with the amino groups of l-cysteine functionalized MoOxS2-x QDs via EDC/NHS coupling chemistry. Firstly, the carboxylic group of the thioketal linker-CPT was activated by adding 100 μL EDC and 25 mg NHS in 5 mL DMSO, which reacted with 5 mg of CPT-TK-COOH. The mixture was then stirred in the dark at ambient temperature for half an hour. Afterward, 30 mL of a 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs solution was added and gently stirred at ambient temperature for 24 h. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was further dialyzed using 1 KDa (MWCO) to remove unreacted CPT derivatives. Finally, the supernatant solution of MoOxS2-xCPT was freeze-dried, and the known mass of MoOxS2-xCPT was used to determine the drug-loaded content and encapsulation efficiency by using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy (λ = 366 nm), by relating the calibration curve (R2 = 0.98).

CPT drug release experiments were carried out under different pH values and laser stimulation conditions using 250 mL of PBS. In a brief, 5 mL MoOxS2-xCPT solution (100 ppm) was sealed in the dialysis bag, immersed in PBS solution, and dialyzed for 48 h under fixed conditions (400 rpm and 37 °C). The dialysis was done at different pH levels (5.0, and 7.4), to investigate the influence of pH on drug release. This step was repeated in the presence of a laser (532 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) and hydrogen peroxide at various pH values to evaluate ROS's role in the drug-releasing effect. At predetermined time intervals, 1 mL of sample solution was taken from the dialysis medium (replaced with an equal amount of fresh PBS to maintain the total volume of the outside phase constant) and the absorbance at λ = 366 nm was measured using UV–Vis spectroscopy. The concentration of CPT released at each time interval was estimated by relating the calibration curve (R2 = 0.98).

Two different probe tests were conducted to examine the effects of MoOxS2-x QDs on the production of 1O2 in the first test, a probe molecule called 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used to examine the production of ROS. DPBF was mixed with MoOxS2-x QDs in dimethyl sulfoxide and placed in a quartz cuvette. The mixture was then exposed to 532 nm laser radiation at a power density of 1.0 W/cm2 for 5 min intervals, repeated for a total of 45 min. The absorption spectra of DPBF were measured using UV–Vis spectroscopy at a wavelength of 416 nm to observe changes related to the production of singlet oxygen (1O2). The conversion of DPBF into DBB was noted, indicating the presence of 1O2 in the system. A control experiment was also conducted using DPBF under laser irradiation for comparison.

In the second probe test, a mixture of MoOxS2-x QDs in an aqueous solution (320 μL, 100 ppm) and a DMA/DMF solution (80 μL) was injected into the quartz cuvette and irradiated with a laser 532 nm laser at a power density of 1.0 W/cm2 for over 5 min intervals, repeated for a total of 50 min. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of DMA was monitored using a fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

Furthermore, the formation of singlet oxygen in the system was detected by measuring the PL spectra in the 1150–1350 nm region. Briefly, 0.3 mg of MoOxS2-x QDs were dissolved in 3 mL of water, and purged with oxygen gas for 600 s. The mixture was then exposed to a 532 nm laser for 10 min, and the emission spectra were captured using photoluminescence spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-enhanced peroxidase activities of MoOxS2-x QDs
ce:section.ce:para: This study aimed to investigate the peroxidase activity of MoOxS2-x QDs in a Fenton-like reaction by varying the pH of the solution. The dynamics of •OH radical generation were also studied by varying concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs and H2O2. To monitor the age of •OH, a combination of TMB and H2O2 solutions was employed, and the mixture was irradiated with a 532 nm laser at a power density of 1.0 W/cm2. In the experiment, a mixture of, 50 μL of TMB solution (10 mM in DMSO), 75 μL of H2O2 (50 mM) and 250 μL PBS (pH = 5.0) was prepared in a quartz cuvette. Then, 50 μL of MoOxS2-x QDs (100 ppm) was added to the mixture, and the cuvette was irradiated with a 532 nm, 1.0 W/cm2 laser for 5 min intervals. The formation of oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) was measured at 652 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer [35].

Furthermore, the type of ROS generated by MoOxS2-x QDs was examined using electron spin resonance (ESR). The experiment used the radical trappers 100 mM TEMP (in DMSO) and 100 mM DMPO (in DMSO and H2O), respectively, to detect 1O2, •O2 –, and •OH radical species. In this experiment, 0.9 mL of MoOxS2-x QDs solution was combined with 0.1 mL of spin trapping (either TEMP or DMPO), and ESR spectra were collected after 5 min of illumination with a 532 nm laser (2.0 W/cm2).

Using Ellman's assay, the capacity of MoOxS2-x QDs to deactivate GSH substrate was evaluated [36]. The thiol group of GSH can interact with the Mo+IV/VI ion to convert into GSSG intermediates with an absorbance peak of about 412 nm. To perform the assay, 250 μL of MoOxS2-x QDs were added to 200 μL of PBS and 50 μL of GSH (1 mM, pH = 5.0). Subsequently, 50 μL of the previous solution was combined with 0.2 μL of DTNB (50 mM in DMSO), and the absorption spectra were measured using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy at 10 min intervals. The effects depended on both time and the concentration of MoOxS2-x QDs.

To investigate the biocompatibility of MoOxS2-x QDs at the cellular level, human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells were first cultivated in EMEME medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% nonessential amino acids, 1% anti-biotic antimycotic formulation, 1% sodium pyruvate, and 1% l-glutamine at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. For mammary carcinoma breast (4T1) cell lines, RPMI-1640 medium composed of 2 mM l-glutamine, 10 mM HEPES, 1 mM sodium pyruvate, 4500 mg/L glucose, 1500 mg/L sodium bicarbonate, and 10% fetal bovine serum was used. The cells were then incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cytotoxicity of MoOxS2-x QDs was assessed using WST-1 assays. In a 96-well plate, combinations of 4T1 cells (∼1.0 ×105) and 100 μL medium were cultured and fixed at 37 0C in a humidified 5% CO2. After 24 h, the medium was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS before adding a fresh culture medium containing different concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs solution (0–500 μg/mL). The cells were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h and 48 h. Next to that, the old medium was replaced with a fresh medium and 0.01 mL of WST-1 reagent was introduced to the cells. The plate was incubated for another 30 min. Viable cell counting was demonstrated by measuring the absorbance at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Bio-Tek Rad). The same procedure was repeated for HeLa cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH at intracellular level
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, a modified version of a previously described technique was used to detect intracellular GSH depletion [36 37 38]. 4T1 cells were seeded in 24 well culture plates at a density of 1×105 cells per well in 2.0 mL of DMEM and incubated for 24 h. The cells developed in DMEM devoid of QDs were designated as the control group, while fresh DMEM containing QDs in a range of concentrations (from 0 to 100 ppm) was utilized to replace the original medium. After a 12 h incubation period, the cells were washed with PBS and detached using trypsin. The cells were then suspended in 0.8 mL of PBS, placed on ice (-4 °C), and centrifuged for 5 min. at 10,000 rpm. After removing the supernatant, the cells were resuspended in 1 mL of PBS and lysed by freezing and thawing thrice. Afterwards, 500 μL of the cell homogenates were taken, and 2 μL of the DTNB reagent (50 mM DTNB, the GSH assay kit) was added. After being centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was collected for GSH tests. In a 96-well plate, 100 μL of the prepared sample with the reagent was added to all the tested wells. Parallel additions of a control group reagent and a series of blank reagents were likewise made to the tested wells. Meanwhile, standard groups of 100 μL out of 500 μL of GSH standard solution (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM) and 2 μL of DNTB reagent were established. After well mixing and waiting 5 min, the optical density (OD) values at 412 nm of each well were measured using a Bio-Tek microplate absorption spectrometer. Plotting a series of GSH-added concentrations versus OD resulted in the creation of the standard calibration graph. The proportion of GSH detected in cells following treatment with prodrug QDs was then calculated, as shown in the equation [36 37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptaking and invitro fluorescence imaging experiment
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the intracellular uptaking of MoOxS2-x QDs by cancer cells, 4T1 cancer cells (1×105 cell density) were grown overnight in a 6-well plate. Subsequently, the cancer cells were washed with PBS before adding 2 mL of medium containing various doses of MoOxS2-x QDs. After 24 h, the cells were rinsed again in PBS before being treated with 2 mL of 70% alcohol for 10 min. Then, 2 mL of NucGreen dye was used to stain the cellular nucleus structure. The cells were then carefully fixed onto a glass slide after being washed twice with PBS. Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy was used to capture fluorescent images of cancer cells.

To examine the production of ROS within cells, DCFH-DA was used. 4T1 cells were grown overnight on 6-well cell chamber slides. Several MoOxS2-x QDs were added to fresh medium to replace the overnight media. After 6 h of incubation, DCFH-DA (25 μM) was added, and after 20 min, the system was either left in the dark or exposed to laser light at 532 nm, 1.00 W/cm2, for 5 min, to start the formation of singlet oxygen (1O2). After washing with PBS, they were treated with 70% alcohol (2 mL) for 20 min, and then washed again with PBS. Finally, the cells were stained with DAPI (2 ppm), and fluorescence images were captured using confocal laser scanning microscopy. To promote the formation of the hydroxyl radical (•OH), which is frequently utilized to generate more ROS in the system, we followed the same process but added H2O2 to the medium containing the quantum dots.

Using 4T1 cells as a model, the ability of MoOxS2-x QD and MoOxS2-xCPT to treat cancer was tested. The cells were first planted at a density of 1×105 cells per well in 6-well plates, and then treated with non-encapsulated (MoOxS2-x QDs) and encapsulated (MoOxS2-xCPT) materials to confirm the effectiveness of the MoOxS2-x QDs and the synergistic impact of encapsulated MoOxS2-xCPT for cancer treatment. After rinsing with PBS, fresh media containing propidium iodide (PI, 1 mM) was added. The cells were then exposed to a 532 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 5 min to initiate the generation of ROS in the system. The cells were further treated for 20 min to react with ROS-produced dead cells stained with PI. Finally, the non-internalized cells were removed with PBS, and the cells were fixed with 2 mL of 70% alcohol for 20 min. Finally, the fixed cells were stained with Nucgreen (2 mL, 10 mM in PBS) for 20 min, and the fluorescence signals from the stained cells were quantified using confocal laser scanning microscopy.

For intracellular •OH generation test, 4T1 cells were seeded into 6-well plates for 24 h. The culture medium was then replaced with medium containing 100 μM H2O2, and MoOxS2-x QDs or MoOxS2-xCPT were added, followed by exposure to a laser to stimulate the photo-induced generation of hydroxyl radical in system. The cells were then cultured with PI (1 μM) for 20 min, and the nuclear stain was fixed with NucGreen (2 mL, 10 mM in PBS). Normal cultured cells were used as control samples. After 2 h, the cells were washed with PBS, and the intracellular •OH detection was evaluated using confocal laser scanning microscopy images.

Cancerous 4T1 cells were cultured in a six-well plate for a day. After that, the cells were then pre-incubated with a culture media containing several inhibitors, such as methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MeβCD, 16 mM), genistein (100 μg/mL), nystatin (50 μg/mL), and amiloride hydrochloride (2.5 mM) for 1 h. After removing the medium, the cells were incubated with MoOxS2-xCPT for 3 h. The cells were stained with NucGreen according to the protocol, and the internalization of MoOxS2-xCPT into the cells was tracked using CLSM.

The absorption and emission properties of the prepared MoOxS2-x QDs were studied using a steady state Ultraviolet–Visible spectrometer (Model: FP-6500; manufacturer: JASCO) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (Model: Fluorolog-3; manufacturer: HORIBA Jobin Yvon). Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL; model NanoHarp, Pico Quantity) was also used to analyze the decay lifetime of the MoOxS2-x QDs; while the morphological study was carried out by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; model Tecnai F20G2 FETEM, Philips: model JEM-2100F, JEOL). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using an ESCALAB 250 (VG Scientific), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Bruker D8 Discover diffractometer equipped with monochromatized Cu Ka radiation (wavelength 1.54 A0). The surface characterization of the obtained QDs was done using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer (thermo electron, USA). Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR) was conducted to confirm the conformation of thiolinker (TK-COOH) and CPT-TK-COOH formation. The time-resolved PL decay lifetime was recorded using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique, containing a Jobin-Yvon H10 monochromator and picosecond pulsed diode laser (PicoQuant PDL 200-B) at 450 nm wavelength. The PL lifetimes were analyzed using the PicoQuant FluoFit program with the low reduced chi-square value (1.0 < X2 < 1.2). The laser irradiated experiment was conducted using a 532 nm laser source (Led & Tec, MGL-III-532-100mW, Hi-Tech Optoelectronics Co., Ltd. Beijing, China).

The aqueous-based synthesis of MoOxS2-x QDs was conducted using a bottom-up approach at room temperature via a straightforward chemical reaction involving MoO3 (Mo precursor), l-cysteine, and Na2S (sulfide precursor) with bovine serum albumin (BSA) used as a surfactant as shown in Scheme 1 . In contrast to the previously reported synthetic approach [12 39], we designed a rapid and facile aqueous-based biomineralization method for MoOxS2-x QD at room temperature. Inspired by biomineralization, natural biopolymers, BSA were used as surfactants because of their template effect and excellent biocompatibility [12 40 41]. During the synthesis, the pH value controls the decomposition of the Mo-precursor solution to stable MoO4 2- (Scheme 1) at pH above 10. Then, MoO4 2- solution was introduced into the BSA solution and accompanied by the frequent addition of Na2S and l-cysteine solution in basic condition (pH = 7.75 + 0.25) to activate sulfide precursor. The as-synthesized product, MoOxS2-x QDs, was obtained with remarkable optical QYs of 37.28% (Table 1), which has been rare in TMD-based QDs and exceptionally higher than that of monolayer 2D MoS2 nanosheets, bulk MoS2, and previously reported MoS2 QDs (Table S5).

The structural morphology and composition of the as-synthesized MoOxS2-x QDs were determined using TEM and XPS. The TEM images in Fig. 1 a showed a monodisperse particle morphology of the MoOxS2-x QDs, and the size distribution was further analyzed from TEM picture using ImageJ software and estimated to be ∼ 4.3 nm (Fig. S3a). In addition, MoOxS2-
x QDs were further analyzed using high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), proving the formation of spherically designed particles (Fig. 1b) with a narrow size distribution. The interplanar distance of MoOxS2-x QDs was retrieved from the HRTEM picture, as shown in Fig. 1c. It clearly demonstrated a highly crystalline nature with a lattice spacing of 0.234 nm, corresponding to (100) lattice planes (Fig. 1d). The average diameter of produced MoOxS2-x QDs (4.35 nm) is equivalent to the bulk Bohr exciton diameter of roughly 4 nm [42]. The EDS spectra confirmed the presence for compositional ingredients of MoOxS2-x QDs; as illustrated on Fig. 1e.

The XPS analysis was also performed to examine the composition and the corresponding chemical valence state of the elements in MoOxS2-x QDs. The survey XPS spectra (Fig. 1f) show characteristic peaks at 65.01 eV and 500.0 eV ascribed to the presence of Mo (4p) and Mo (3d), respectively. The peaks for S (2p), O (1s), N (1s), and C (1s) were observed at 200.0, 532.5, 400.0, and 287.3 eV, respectively, verifying the presence of main ingredient precursors in the MoOxS2-x QDs. Briefly, magnified XPS observations proved the co-existence of different Mo species such as; Mo+VI at 235.2 eV, Mo+IV 3d3/2 at 231.8 eV, and Mo+IV 3d5/2 at 227.3 eV (Fig. 1g), which is consistent with recent reports [43]. The O (1s) spectra resulted two deconvoluted peaks at 534.2 eV and 531.2 eV, confirming the presence of Mo-O and C-OH on the surface of the MoOxS2-x QDs (Fig. 1h). Fig. 1i showed the deconvoluted peaks for S (2p) resulting with the S 2p1/2 and S 2P3/2 peaks at 163.2 eV and 159.2 eV, which are consistent with the bivalent oxidation state of sulfur. In addition, the binding energy at 169.1 eV corresponds to S (VI) and the orbitals of divalent sulfide ions. N (1s) spectra was integrated into two peaks at 400.2 and 402.8 eV related to –NH2 and protonated NH3 +, respectively (Fig. 1j). The deconvoluted peaks for C (1s) contain CO, CC, and CO or CS environment at 290.0, 286.1, and 283.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 1k). The structure of MoOxS2-
x QDs was further characterized by powder X-ray diffractometry (Fig. S3b). The XRD pattern of MoOxS2-
x QDs showed a low-dimensional crystal structure, which mainly suggests the small grain sizes of the QDs. The main peaks at 11.5°, 28°, 31.5°, and 46° can be assigned to the characteristic signal of (100), (001), (112), and (321) lattice planes (standard reference Na2Mo3S4 (PDF 51–0948)).

The surface functional groups on QDs were investigated through FTIR. As shown in Fig. S4, the peaks at 3650, 3286, 2958, 935, 639, and 437 cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching vibration of –NH, COOH/C-OH, CH, SH, Mo-O, and Mo-S, indicating the successful preparation of MoOxS2-x QDs. This result could be further verified by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (Fig. 1e). Zeta potentials of the as-synthesized QDs were measured to investigate the surface charge of QDs. Typically, the surface charges of QDs fluctuated around zero at negative, due to the charge neutralizing l-cysteine on the surface of the QDs (Fig. S3c), further suggesting good biocompatibility of QDs. We hypothesized that the divalent sulfide ion group found on the L- cysteine first formed a charge-charge interaction with MoVI on the surfaces of the colloidal QDs. In alkaline, SH (l-cysteine) was deprotonated into S- accompanied by generation of (S--MoVI) ion intercalation [44]. Notably, the PL intensity of MoOxS2-x QDs was clearly observed with only minor change with increasing pH from 4 to 11 (Figure S4 c and d), suggesting the insensitivity of QDs to pH variations. The colloidal suspension of MoOxS2-x QDs exhibited long-term stability with no conspicuous aggregations for over 1 month and at room temperature (25–27 °C). Similarly, the stability of QDs in water, PBS, DMEM and NaCl did not show any aggregation for over two weeks or no significant reduction of PL intensity (Fig. S5a − 5e). This long-term stability and luminescence properties of MoOxS2-x QDs are beneficial in biomedical applications [12].

The optical behavior of MoOxS2-x QDs was studied using UV–vis absorption and PL spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectra of MoOxS2-x QDs in Fig. 2 a displayed distinct peaks at 300 and 400 nm and a long tail absorption peak in the region between 400 and 550 nm. The broad absorption peak near 300 nm may be due to surface adsorbed functional groups of MoOxS2-x QDs (–NH2 and –COOH) and excitonic features of monodispersed QDs, which is consistent with previously reported literature [45 46]. Furthermore, the existence of the Mo-S bond with l-cysteine results in a dramatic decrease in the lateral diameters of MoOxS2-x QDs, which contributes to a stronger quantum size impact and the 400 nm absorption maximum. This phenomenon mainly promotes metal-to-ligand charge transfer and produces band absorption around 400–500 nm due to ligand-to-metal charge transfer (n – π*) of Mo-O and M−S bonds and Mo, O, and S related surface defects. The PL spectra of as-prepared MoOxS2-x QDs were measured with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and a maximum emission at 450 nm was recorded as shown in Fig. 2a. Firstly, the effects of sulfur-containing additives and stabilizers on the PL emission were systematically studied. With increasing S content (Na2S), the PL emission intensity of MoOxS2-x QDs at 450 nm continually increased up to a 1:1.5 (Mo:S) ratio mainly due to elimination of defect structures, and then start to dropped with overflowing of S ratio, as shown in Fig. 2b and Table S1. This drop mainly occurred due to the decrease in S defects in these MoOxS2-x QDs. Similarly, the molar ratio of l-cysteine in MoOxS2-x QDs was also optimized under basic environment of l-cysteine, as illustrated in Fig. 2c and Table S2. Furthermore, the optical quantum yield of MoOxS2-x QDs was further investigated by altering the reaction variables, such as pH (Table S3) and reaction time at room temperature (Fig. S4a). As shown in Fig. 2e, the PL spectra blue-emissive MoOxS2-x QDs were collected using different wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 420 nm, confirming their excitation wavelength-independent PL properties and the maximum PL intensity was obtained at 360 nm. For the record, we obtained defect-rich MoOxS2-x QDs with the highest QYs 37.28% (Table 1), using quinin sulfate reference solution. The PL decay was carried out at room temperature to gain more insights into the photophysical behaviors of QDs, as shown in Fig. 2f. A 360 nm excitation wavelength was used to capture the time-resolved PL spectra. The decay profiles fitted according to the first exponential function (Eq. (1)
