)
where ‘I(t)’ is the photon intensity; ‘A’ is the amplitude, and τ is the decay time. The time-resolved PL curves of MoOxS2-x QDs vary with different ratio of Mo:S (Fig. 2f) and Table S4. The results confirmed the average lifetime of MoOxS2-x QDs at various Mo:S feeding ratio of 1:1, 1:1.25, and 1:1.5 was extra-calculated and found to be 2.5, 5.82, and 13.17 ns, respectively (Table S4), which exceeded the direct exciton recombination lifetime of monolayer MoS2 sheets [45 47].

The MoOxS2-x QDs exhibit long-term stability at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 2g and 2h, and no significant aggregation were observed after one-month of storage. The MoOxS2-x QDs showed higher photostability after being subjected to UV-irradiation for 120 min (Fig. 2i). The former exhibited less than 5% for 1:1.5; Mo:S ratio while 1:1.25 exhibited less than 7%. The PL stability was also conducted in several biological solvents (Fig S5), indicating that MoOxS2-x QDs possess good stability and might be used in biological applications. Implying that Na+ intercalation and •OH etching; MoOxS2-x QDs treated by NH4OH and NaCO3 exhibited almost no fluorescence, indicating the positive influence and functions of •OH on sulfur loss during alkaline treatment formation (Fig. S4b). The availability of l-cysteine in MoOxS2-x QDs not only improves QYs and stability but also facilitates anticancer drug conjugation onto the surface of QDs through amino and carboxylic acid groups for cancer treatment activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo/chemodynamic therapy and gas therapy of MoOxS2-x QDs

ce:section.ce:para:
Singlet oxygen (1O2) detection: - As shown in Fig. 3 a, MoOxS2-x QDs are ideal candidates for inducing large amounts of ROS production under light irradiation and are used as photosensitizers in light-activated therapeutic activities such as PDT I or PDT II. In particular, in PDT, the photosensitizer absorbs the light and excites it to the triplet state, which endorses the decomposition of molecular oxygen into singlet oxygen (1O2) [48 49 50]. Therefore, we investigated the suitability of MoOxS2-x QDs for use as a photosensitizer by measuring ROS generation upon laser irradiation. To provide a more reliable and precise result on whether or not ROS were generated, we studied the photochemically related ROS generation capacity and mechanism of MoOxS2-x QDs using specific probe tests, such as the DPBF probe test for 1O2, TMB reagents for •OH, and NBT probe tests for •O2, as shown in Figs. 3 -5
.

The colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene (DBB) is produced when ROS interacts with the yellowish fluorescent DPBF, and the results were confirmed using UV–vis absorption spectra at 412 nm. As shown in Fig. 3b, the UV–vis absorption spectra of DPBF gradually decreased as the laser irradiation time increased in the presence of MoOxS2-x QDs, demonstrating that MoOxS2-x QDs could be used as an effective ROS-generating photosensitizing agent. In contrast, the absorption spectra of DPBF remained unchanged after 45 min of laser irradiation without MoOxS2-x QDs (Fig. 3c). Almost 55% of the DPBF was decreased in the presence of MoOxS2-x QDs while remaining unchanged in the absence of material under laser irradiation (Fig. 3c). Similarly, we employed 9,10-dimethylanthracene (DMA) probe to confirm the formation of singlet oxygen using MoOxS2-x QDs under laser irradiation. As observed in Fig. 3d, the maximum emission peak at 434 nm corresponding to the DMA was quenched as the laser irradiation time increased, suggesting that a significant amount of ROS was effectively generated. The findings showed that the PL emission peak of pure DMA didn't decline after repeated laser irradiations in the absence of material, despite 80% of the original PL intensity declining when MoOxS2-x QDs were incubated with DMA (Fig. 3e).

Furthermore, the 1O2 generation efficacy of MoOxS2-x QDs validated with PL emission spectra was observed near 1271 nm (λexcitation = 532 nm). After 10 min of O2 gas saturation, the MoOxS2-x QDs sample was exposed to a 532 nm laser pulse, and the PL spectra were then captured at λexcitation = 532 nm. The presence of an intense PL peak with a bandwidth of 20.8 nm was seen at 1271 nm (Fig. 3f), which strongly supports the formation of 1O2 radicals.

In PDT mechanism, the MoOxS2-x QDs absorb light, and the excited electrons transform into the triplet state via intersystem crossing (ISC) mechanism, where they catalyze the breakdown of molecular oxygen/water into ROS, as depicted in Fig. 3a. As highlighted in Fig. 3a, MoOxS2-x QDs are designed as oxygen-self-sufficient material to overcome hypoxia and achieve highly effective PDT in solid tumors. Thus, the excited triplet state (T1) activates the formation of ROS using two different pathways: electron-transfer reaction, where the water molecule is transformed into •OH and •O2 – radicals (PDT II); and energy-transfer process, which catalyzes the formation of 1O2 radical from molecular O2 (PDT I), promoting ROS-generation. During a photodynamic process, the excited singlet state (S1) absorbs the photon energy by quantum dots under excitation with an appropriate wavelength of light, which is then transferred to the excited triplet state (T1) vi the ISC, while So, S1, and Ssur in Fig. 3a inset respectively ground state, excited state of MoOxS2-x QDs core, and surface state in different dynamic relation process.

Hydroxyl radical (•OH) detection:- It has been claimed that a pH-regulated environment and photothermal based photo-electric stimuli could be used to hydrolyze H2O2 to lethal •OH through a Fenton-like reaction for chemodynamic therapy (CDT) with reduced toxicity [51 36,52]. As shown in Fig. 4a, MoOxS2-x QDs have the potential to be used in the peroxidase-like and GSH depletion reactions due to the tunable oxidation state of Mo+IV/VI ion. In this experiment, TMB enzymatic tests were used to evaluate the production of •OH radicals. In the presence of •OH radicals, the chromophore substrate, TMB, undergoes an oxidation pathway that results in oxidized TMB (ox-TMB), identified by UV–vis spectroscopy at 652 nm wavelength. As displayed in Fig. 4b and 4c, the photothermal-based photoelectric effects, Mo(IV)/Mo(VI) ion-induced decomposition of H2O2 into •OH radicals at various pH conditions, and concentration-dependent ox-TMB formation was observed. As the pH of the reaction media became increasingly acidic, the absorbance spectrum of ox-TMB at 652 nm gradually increased, suggesting the production of tumor-responsive •OH radicals. Similarly, the formation of ox-TMB was augmented by the acidic pH as the concentration of MoOxS2-x QDs increased, suggesting that the phenomena was pH and concentration-dependent. In the presence of H2O2, MoOxS2-x QDs accelerated the oxidation of TMB to generate a characteristic blue hue through a Fenton-like reaction, but no color change was detected when MoOxS2-x QDs were excluded from the mixture (Fig. 4d). Additionally, by adopting time and H2O2 concentration variables (Fig. 4e-f), we discovered pronounced absorption spectra with increasing incubation time and concentration related to ox-TMB formation, proving the •OH generation is time dependent. These data suggest that MoOxS2-x QDs might be attractive candidates for use as a CDT agent in cancer therapy.

GSH depletion property: - On the other hand, GSH is a potent intracellular antioxidant that can scavenge ROS produced both extracellularly and intracellularly, leading to an exacerbated hypoxic environment and reduced efficiency of ROS-based therapies [37 38 53 54]. Therefore, controlling GSH activity can significantly enhance the efficiency of CDT and decrease oxidative damage, especially at the intracellular level [55]. The ability of MoOxS2-x QDs to deactivate the GSH reagent was investigated in a mildly acidic environment (pH 5.0), where Mo+VI/MoIV ions interacted with the GSH solution, and released yellowish Mo(IV)-TNB, which has an absorption band at 412 nm (Fig. 4g). This was then coupled with the DTNB molecule to generate GSSG (Fig. 4a). Fig. 4h demonstrated that the absorption spectra of the GS-TNB intermediate at 412 nm declined as the incubation time of MoOxS2-x QDs with GSH progressed, suggesting that the levels of GSH were effectively degraded or oxidized by MoOxS2-x QDs. The experiment was repeated using a concentration gradient of MoOxS2-x QDs, verifying that the absorption spectra of GSH declined with increasing concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs, indicating that GSH consumption was greatly favored at high concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs (Fig. S6a and S6b). Fig. 4i summarizes the GSH depleting activity with and without MoOxS2-x QDs, revealing that the absorbance spectra at 412 nm significantly declined in the presence of 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs, whereas it remained unchanged in the absence of MoOxS2-x QDs. These findings are consistent with previous explanations. Overall, the oxidizing ability of MoOxS2-x QDs was manipulated by varying the substrate concentration and incubation time, as shown in Fig. S6b and Fig. 4h. The formation of reduced GSH simultaneously released high-content Mo(IV) ions, which are favorable for H2O2 decomposition, and hence enhance the peroxidase activity of MoOxS2-x QDs.

Superoxide radical (•O2 –) detection: - The ROS produced from the light-activated MoOxS2-x QDs were further analyzed using NBT colorimetric assays to validate the presence of •O2 – radical species. The UV–vis spectra of pure NBT or incubated with MoOxS2-x QDs did not show significant changes at 260 nm under laser irradiation and longer incubation time (Fig. 5a − 5c), approving the absence of •O2
– radicals in our system.

Using ESR spectroscopy, the MoOxS2-x QDs' capacity to produce ROS was further qualitatively investigated. For these purposes, TEMP and DMPO were employed as probes to find 1O2 and •O2 –/•OH, respectively. In the presence of the laser, MoOxS2-x QDs containing TEMP produced a set of characteristic TEMP-1-Oxyl (TEMP) signal peaks with a ratio of 1:1:1, and this behavior became noticeably more prominent and was ascribed to the production of 1O2 (Fig. 5d). Similar results were obtained when •OH radicals were detected using DMPO in H2O. First, as shown in Fig. 5e, neither the DMPO/H2O2 solution with the laser alone nor the MoOxS2-x QDs/H2O2 solution without the laser produced any signals. But the ESR spectra of DMPO and MoOxS2-x QDs/H2O2 solutions showed signal peaks at a 1:2:2:1 ratio due to the formation of DMPOO adducts under laser irradiation. This showed that the as-synthesized quantum dots produced light-induced •OH radicals. According to the patterns above, neither the MoOxS2-x QDs/H2O2 experiment nor the comparison experiment (DMPO in DMSO) produced an ESR signal whether the laser was present or not (Fig. 5f). According to the results of ESR spectroscopy and colorimetric assay, the most frequent radical species created by the MoOxS2-x QDs were 1O2 and •OH, whereas no detectable •O2 – was produced in the system. A schematic illustration was proposed, as shown in Fig. 3a, to help explain the mechanism of effective ROS generation from MoOxS2-x QDs and the multiple exciton property of molybdenum-oxo-sulfide quantum dots.

Due to its gaseous state at normal temperatures and strong reactivity in biological systems, H2S presents a challenge for direct medicinal applications [56 57]. The two substances most frequently producing H2S today are sodium sulfide and sodium hydrosulfide. Unfortunately, these inorganic sulphide sources quickly increase H2S concentrations after breaking down, which may lead to remarkably high local H2S concentrations that are harmful to normal cell [57]. We fabricated MoOxS2-x QDs as an alternative biomimetic H2S generating platform using bioinorganic sulfide source to overcome this challenge. Thus, we explored the H2S generation properties of MoOxS2-x QDs owing to the high sulfide content for gas therapy of cancer. In this examination, we adopted the typical methylene blue (MB) method to track the release of H2S gas and N, N-dimethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DMPD) was used as a probe detector (Fig. S6c and 6d). As shown in Fig. 5g, the color of the mixture solution (DMPD/ MoOxS2-x QDs) was gradually transformed to blue owing to the formation of MB+ dye as observed in Fig. S6c. Moreover, the H2S generation MoOxS2-x QDs was further studied at different pH conditions, showing that the absorbance reading at 664 nm was rapidly growing at pH 5.0, whereas it remained constant at normal pH 7.4 (Fig. 5h). The data show that the generation kinetics (pH 5.0) was accelerated in the first 50 min and after that showed steady generation performance up to 300 min. The findings revealed that the H2S production properties of MoOxS2-x QDs depend on the concentration and pH of the solution. According to the results and the standard quantification curve (R2 = 0.98) in Fig. 5i, MoOxS2-x QDs may be used as a reliable H2S gas generator for cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of MoOxS2-xCPT and ROS-responsive drug release

ce:section.ce:para: The MoOxS2-xCPT conjugate was synthesized primarily in two steps. The first step was synthesizing ROS-sensitive CPT-TK-COOH, and the second was conjugating CPT-TK-COOH with MoOxS2-xCPT using EDC/NHS as a cross-linker. Then, CPT was reacted with TK-COOH to yield CPT-TK-COOH, which was then reacted with MoOxS2-x QDs to yield MoOxS2-xCPT, as shown in Scheme 1. The successful preparation of TK-COOH and CPT-TK-COOH was confirmed by 1H NMR and FTIR spectra (as shown in Figs S1, S2, respectively). For the reaction of CPT-TK-COOH with MoOxS2-x QDs, first CPT-TK-COOH reacted with EDC/NHS as shown in Scheme 1, and then the activated carboxylate group of the thioketal linker was conjugated with the amino group of MoOxS2-x QDs to purify MoOxS2-xCPT. The successful loading of CPT was verified by the UV–vis spectrum, PL emission spectrum, Zeta potential analysis, TEM image, and FTIR spectrum after unbound drug molecules had been removed using 1 KDA (MWCO) dialysis bag and purified.

In contrast to the prodrug MoOxS2-xCPT, CPT-TK-COOH shows a distinct peak in the UV–vis spectrum at around 365–370 nm that was blue shifted to around the 358–365 nm regions, with an additional long tail peak shown in 400–480 nm for MoOxS2-xCPT (Fig. 6 a and 6b), indicating effective drug loading with materials. Notably, the significant shift in fluorescence of MoOxS2-xCPT compared to pure CPT-TK-COOH and the broadening of the MoOxS2-xCPT peak at 460 nm with a full-width half-maximum (FWHM) of 83.60 nm, suggested encapsulation between MoOxS2-x QDs and CPT-TK-COOH. The TEM image in Fig. 6d, indicates that the MoOxS2-xCPT has been fabricated in the form of a circular, surface-coated MoOxS2-x QDs prodrug particles, with a particle size of MoOxS2-xCPT (10.5 ± 0.3 nm). Zeta potential measurements were carried out to examine the charge distribution on the surface of the MoOxS2-x QDs and its prodrug, MoOxS2-xCPT, which also suggests strong interactions between MoOxS2-x QDs and CPT-TK-COOH (Fig. 6f). As shown in Fig. 6f, the MoOxS2-x QDs showed a negative charge around zero (-0.04 mV), while the MoOxS2-xCPT showed a more negative charge of −11.96 mV. The carboxylate group of CPT-TK-COOH, which enhances the negative surface charge of the prodrug after conjugating with MoOxS2-x QDs, indicates the amino group of the quantum dot (its positive charge character) is reduced while the surface negative charge dominates. These results confirm the powerful interactions between MoOxS2-x QDs and CPT-TK-COOH via the amino and carboxylate groups.

Additionally, XPS analysis was performed to examine the elemental composition and corresponding formation of amide bonds during the encapsulation of MoOxS2-x QDs with CPT-TK-COOH, resulting in the formation of MoOxS2-xCPT. The survey XPS spectra (Fig. 6f) display characteristic peaks at 62.67 eV and 497.21 eV ascribed to the presence of Mo (4p) and Mo (3d), respectively. The peaks for S (2p), C (1s), N (1s), and O (1s) were observed at approximately 198.6, 287.0, 400.0, and 530.5 eV, respectively, verifying the presence of main ingredient precursors in the MoOxS2-xCPT. Specifically, enlarged XPS observations of Mo+VI at 234.2 eV, Mo+IV 3d3/2 at 230.4 eV, and Mo+IV 3d5/2 at 225.4 eV demonstrated the coexistence of several Mo species (Fig. 1g), which is consistent with the precursor and recent reports [43]. The presence of CO and CO on the surface of the MoOxS2-xCPT was confirmed by the O (1s) spectra, which produced two deconvoluted peaks at 534.7 eV and 530.4 eV (Fig. 1h). The S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 peaks at 162.3 eV and 160.2 eV, respectively, which are consistent with the bivalent oxidation state of sulfur, were obtained from the deconvoluted peaks for S (2p) shown in Fig. 1i. Furthermore, the splitting of the S (VI) orbitals corresponds to the binding energy of 166.7 eV. N (1s) spectra showed a strong single peak at 398.6 eV (Fig. 6j), which was caused by an amide bond (N-CO) between the amine of the quantum dots and the carboxylic thioketal linker of the drug. Other –NH2 and –NH3 + peaks (400.2 and 402.8 eV, respectively, Fig. 1j) on the surface of MoOxS2-x QDs vanished due to the conversion of the quantum dot amine families to amide bonds. The deconvoluted peaks for C (1s) contain CO, CC, and CS environments at 290.4, 285.5, and 283.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 6k), while an additional peak is observed at 289.0 eV due to the presence of N-CO (amide) synthesis.

The surface functional groups on MoOxS2-xCPT were investigated through FTIR, which revealed medium peaks at 3650 cm−1 (amide stretch), strong and broad peaks at 3300 cm−1 (OH stretch), carbonyl peaks in the region 1750–1580 cm−1, as well as the presence of Mo-O and Mo-S bonds attributed to the stretching vibration around 637 and 437 cm−1 (Fig. S3d). These peaks suggest the components of MoOxS2-xCPT. Finally, the CPT loading efficiency was measured using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 6b), and the results showed that the drug loading content (DLC) and drug loading efficiency (DLE) were 38.8% and 71.3%, respectively.

The release of drugs from a chemically encapsulated drug delivery system is determined by the strength of the chemical interaction and the effectiveness of breaking the linked bond [58]. The thioketal linkage is an intelligent drug delivery approach that employs ROS-responsive linkers to integrate anticancer drugs and construct a prodrugs platform. For efficient drug delivery and efficacy, the prodrug structure must exhibit better stability until it reaches to the tumor environment, and drug release occurs by activating the TK bond in the drug carrier using external stimuli [58 59]. As discussed in Section 3.2, MoOxS2-x QDs can efficiently generate ROS when activated by laser irradiation or H2O2 solution, which can induce the rapid breakage of the TK-bond and results in photo-triggered ROS-dependent drug release (Scheme 1).

The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 6l) demonstrate that CPT release from MoOxS2-xCPT was carried out under various stimulus conditions, such as laser irradiation, H2O2 supply, and pH variations, which could generate a considerable quantity of ROS and triggers the breakage of the TK bond of MoOxS2-xCPT. CPT release from MoOxS2-xCPT was relatively low at pH 7.4 (9%) and pH 5.0 (21%) without activating stimuli such as laser or H2O2. However, after activating the reaction with H2O2 and laser irradiation separately, CPT release reached 31% and 60%, respectively, owing to the production of ROS via the Fenton reaction and PDT mechanism. Treating the drug carrier with both H2O2 and laser irradiation resulted in more than 79% CPT release, confirming the synergistic effect of CDT and PDT. This effect results in a large amount of ROS (1O2 and •OH), thereby catalyzing TK breakdown to release free CPT molecules. Therefore, our paper offered a platform loaded with highly effective anticancer drugs for effective cancer therapy that may be attractive for future in vivo candidates.

The feasibility of adopting MoOxS2-x QDs in intracellular investigations was primarily validated through cytocompatibility experiments using two cancer cell lines, mammary carcinoma breast (4T1) and human cervical cancer (HeLa). The viability of cells was analyzed using the WST-1 reagent. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a and 7b, the cells were incubated with varied doses of MoOxS2-x QDs (0–500 ppm) for different time intervals (24 and 48 h) in the absence of external stimuli. The WST-1 findings revealed that the percentage of viable cells remained above 90% for both 4T1 and HeLa cells after 48 h of incubation time (Fig. 7a and 7b). Moreover, cancer cells demonstrated remarkable tolerance to our product up to 500 ppm, suggesting that our material exhibits excellent biocompatibility and may be a safer anticancer therapy strategy.

MoOxS2-x QDs have the potential to serve as an effective anticancer treatment due to their highly efficient extracellular therapeutic performance through CDT, PDT, Gas therapy, and chemotherapy, as detailed in section 3. The improved solubility and dispersibility of MoOxS2-xCPT enhance the water solubility of the drug and localization to the tumor area. In this section, we investigated the intracellular effectiveness of MoOxS2-x QDs in the presence of external stimuli such as laser and H2O2. As previously shown in Scheme 1, the loading and releasing of the CPT drug were accomplished using crosslinker association and ROS-cleavable thioketal linkage, respectively. For this analysis, 4T1 cells were incubated with different dosages of MoOxS2-x QDs and MoOxS2-xCPT to evaluate chemotherapeutic and photo-enhanced effectiveness and were subsequently treated with various stimulating agents. As shown in Fig. 7c, the number of viable cells did not reduce in the presence of MoOxS2-x QDs alone. However, the cell viability rapidly dropped to 70% when incubated with MoOxS2-xCPT alone, owing to the TME-activated chemotherapeutic activity of CPT. Moreover, 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs caused 50% of cell death (Fig. 7d) when exposed to laser irradiation (532 nm, 1.0 W/cm−2), attributed to the PDT effect of MoOxS2-x QDs. These findings imply that MoOxS2-x QDs were successfully taken up by cancer cells and produced harmful ROS in the cell organs when exposed to laser irradiation.

Furthermore, the PDT/CDT effects of MoOxS2-x QDs were demonstrated with laser irradiation and H2O2 variables. The cell death was promoted up to 72%, due to the formation of 1O2 and •OH in the system (Fig. 7d). The synergistic effect of PDT and CDT was only 22% higher than PDT alone, suggesting that PDT has a higher killing efficiency than CDT. The results confirmed that MoOxS2-x QDs were safely absorbed by cancer cells and generate toxic ROS upon combination with laser and H2O2 for anticancer activity. We also examined and compared the therapeutic benefits of MoOxS2-xCPT by incubating various doses of MoOxS2-xCPT using 4T1 cancer cells. After incubation, the cells were treated with H2O2 and laser light to assess the synergistic effects of CDT/PDT and ROS-driven chemotherapy mechanisms. According to Fig. 7e, the cell death phenomenon was increased to 84.45% by raising the concentration of MoOxS2-xCPT in the presence of H2O2 and a laser source, confirming the formation of harmful 1O2 and •OH radical species in the system, which also promoted the release of the CPT by breaking the TK bond of MoOxS2-xCPT and inducing chemotherapy efficacy for strong cancer cell eradication. Fig. 7f summarizes the cell-killing efficacy of MoOxS2-xCPT through using alone therapy and their synergistic performance.

On the other hand, GSH-depleting substances may lower intracellular GSH while also increasing ROS, leading to severe oxidative stress in tumor cells [37]. Accordingly, 4T1 cells were cultured with different concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 ppm) for overnight, and the intracellular GSH level was evaluated using DTNB probe reagent. The intracellular GSH level dropped with increasing MoOxS2-x QDs concentration, as shown in Fig. 7g. Due to the reducing ability of Mo(VI, IV), the MoOxS2-x QDs were able to neutralize the GSH composition in tumor cells. After incubation with 100 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs, 78% of intracellular GSH level was consumed, meaning only 22% of the GSH remained in the cells (Fig. 7g and 7h). Thus, MoOxS2-xCPT has a promising multi-therapeutic capacity to be exploited as an anticancer agent in future nanomedicine.

To assess the cellular uptake and intracellular localization of MoOxS2-x QDs, 4T1 cells were chosen as model cells, and the fluorescence images were captured using confocal laser scanning microscopy. Fig. 7i depicts microscopic images of 4T1 cancer cells treated with different concentrations of MoOxS2-x QDs, revealing that the green fluorescence signal from NucGreen indicates the location of cell nuclei, whereas the blue fluorescence signal from MoOxS2-x QDs is dispersed throughout the cytoplasm and nucleus of 4T1 cells. In addition, higher intracellular fluorescence intensities were observed with increasing doses of MoOxS2-x QDs (Fig. 7i). Thus, the dose-dependent results of the cellular uptake efficiency of MoOxS2-x QDs support the hypothesis that the in vitro cell killing efficacy of CT, PDT, and CDT is dependent on the concentration of MoOxS2-x QDs (Fig. 7c – 7e).

After confirming the cellular uptake of MoOxS2-x QDs, the ability of MoOxS2-x QDs to regulate intracellular oxidative stress upon laser irradiation was examined using the DCFH-DA probe. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a and suggested in Fig. 8b, under conditions of intracellular oxidative stress, non-fluorescent DCFH-DA reacts with ROS produced in the cellular organ system to transform into the green fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). DAPI was used to stain the nucleus. According to the CLSM photographs in Fig. 8a, the 4T1 cells treated with a laser and H2O2 only without the MoOxS2-x QDs did not exhibit a green fluorescence signal. However, the cells treated with the laser and MoOxS2-x QDs showed greener fluorescence signals than the control cells, suggesting the generation of intracellular ROS due to the PDT effect of MoOxS2-x QDs. In the L50 channel (Fig. 8a), a weak green fluorescence signal originating from DCF was recorded when 4T1 cells were cultured with 50 ppm MoOxS2-x QDs and irradiated with a 532 nm laser. As the concentration of MoOxS2-x QDs went up, the green fluorescence signal became brighter, confirming that the PDT response was concentration-dependent. To further evaluate ROS generation via peroxidase activity, the green fluorescence signal became stronger with increasing MoOxS2-x QDs concentration, due to the formation of excess •OH radicals generated in the system, which intensified the oxidation of DCFH into a DCF-radical (Fig. 8a, L100 +H channel).

The mechanism of intracellular uptake was further studied using various inhibitors, as shown in Fig. 8c. 4T1 cancer cells were cultured with different inhibitors such as MβCD, nystatin, amiloride, and genistein for 30 min and then incubated with 100 ppm of MoOxS2-xCPT for 3 h. The CLSM images in Fig. 8c demonstrate that the genistein inhibitor significantly suppressed MoOxS2-xCPT uptake, indicating that the caveolae-mediated endocytosis is a viable uptake mechanism for MoOxS2-x QDs. Notably, the amiloride inhibitor facilitated less MoOxS2-xCPT absorption than the MβCD inhibitor, which had approximately the same mean fluorescence intensity (Fig. 8c) as the control experiment (without the inhibitor). This result suggests that the MoOxS2-xCPT is endocytosed via multiple pathways, as described in Fig. 8d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Invitro fluorescence of imaging of therapeutics studies
ce:section.ce:para: Confocal images of 4T1 cells were collected to highlight the quantitative therapeutics data analysis of the material. MoOxS2-x QDs or MoOxS2-xCPT at various concentrations were incubated with cancer cells for overnight and stained with a NucGreen staining agent to monitor the nucleus of the cell structure. The cell death caused by photo-enhanced ROS accumulation was investigated using the PI reagent after laser exposure. The red fluorescent probe molecule called PI interacts with the cytoplasm of dead cell structures in confocal imaging to produce red fluorescence. Due to the absence of MoOxS2-x QDs, the cells treated with either laser or H2O2 (Fig. 9 a) did not emit red fluorescence. Similarly, following the addition of MoOxS2-x QDs (without laser and H2O2), the cells only displayed blue fluorescent (Fig. 9b) due to the blue-emissive property of MoOxS2-x QDs (Fig. S7a and 7b). Also, the PI channel did not show red fluorescence, confirming that the cell-killing effect of MoOxS2-x QDs is stimuli-responsive. However, when combining MoOxS2-x QDs with a laser source, the PI molecule interacted with dead cells and produced red fluorescence due to the PDT effect of MoOxS2-x QDs. In addition, in the presence of laser and H2O2, the confocal photograph displayed bright red spots, indicating that MoOxS2-x QDs caused massive cell destruction owing to the generation of ROS in the cells system via PDT/CDT mechanisms (Fig. 9d). Furthermore, the synergistic effect of MoOxS2-xCPT was investigated by treating cancer cells without and with H2O2 in the presence of laser irradiation (Fig. 9e and 9f), resulting in ultra-fluorescent red images due to the improved killing capability of MoOxS2-xCPT through PDT/CDT/CT as multi-therapeutic modalities. The mean fluorescence intensity of PI signal confocal image corresponding to control, MoOxS2-x QD, PDT, PDT/CDT, PDT/CT, and PDT/CDT/CT calculated from Fig. 9 (PI channel, Fig. S7d) also confirmed the multi-therapeutic modalities.

Herein, we have successfully built an integrated bioengineered multifunctional platform, blue emissive defect-rich MoOxS2-x QDs via rapid biomineralization technique with superior optical quantum yield (QYs) reaching up to 37.28% and exceptional therapeutic benefits for image-guided cancer therapy. The integration of a sulfide precursor and protein template into the MoOxS2-x QDs results in a longer decay lifetime (13.17 ns) and enhances the radiative recombination site responsible for improved optical properties, which has been rare in TMD-based QDs [12 60 46]. Thus, MoOxS2-x QDs efficiently generate reactive oxygen species such as singlet oxygen (1O2) under laser illumination for photodynamic therapy and cytotoxic hydroxyl radical (•OH) in the presence of peroxide solution for chemodynamic therapy via the Fenton reaction. Simultaneously, the intracellular antioxidant, glutathione (GSH), could be effectively depleted via Mo+VI/IV induced, enhancing the efficacy of ROS-mediated therapeutics. A large amount of sulfide composition resulted in the release of H2S gas under acidic conditions for cancer gas therapy. Then, MoOxS2-x QDs was further conjugated with ROS-responsive thioketal linked Camptothecin (CPT-TK-COOH) drug, forming a multitargeted MoOxS2-xCPT anticancer agent with better drug-loading efficiency (38.8%). After activating the ROS generation through CDT and PDT mechanisms, a record of 79% CPT was released in 48 h. In vitro experiments verified that MoOxS2-xCPT possessed higher biocompatibility with 4T1 and HeLa cells but exhibited substantial toxicity in laser/H2O2, resulting in 84.5% cell death through PDT/CDT and chemotherapeutic effects. Therefore, the designed MoOxS2-xCPT coupling the ROS-generator with the stimuli-responsive drug is a potential candidate in future biomedicine and should be considered for further investigation through in vivo studies and clinical trials.

Worku Batu Dirersa: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Girum Getachew: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Aswandi Wibrianto: Visualization, Formal analysis. Akash S. Rasal: Formal analysis. Vivek S. Gurav: Formal analysis. Mochamad Zakki Fahmi: Formal analysis. Jia-Yaw Chang: Supervision, Visualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:section.ce:para: The supplemental data that was downloaded was as follows: 1H NMR and FT-IR spectra of TK-COOH and CPT-TK-COOH, the particle size distribution of MoOxS2-x QDs, the Powder XRD pattern of MoOxS2-x QDs, the Zeta potential of MoOxS2-x QDs, the FTIR spectra of MoOxS2-x QDs, MoOxS2-xCPT, and CPT-TK-COOH, and the PL intensity of MoOxS2-x QDs etched by NaCO3, NH4OH. The tables mentioned in the article are Table S1, Table S2, Table S3, Table S4, and Table S5. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.05.099.
